danish_text_clean: Thomas Lange er direktør, og én gang om året har han lønsamtaler med sine ansatte. Nu har han indvilliget i at fortælle om, hvad han mener, der virker for ansatte generelt – og hvad man skal holde sig fra at nævne.
english_translation: Thomas Lange is a director, and once a year he has salary talks with his employees. Now he has agreed to tell about what he thinks works for employees in general – and what one should refrain from mentioning.

vocabulary.example_from_article: Thomas Lange er direktør, og én gang om året har han lønsamtaler med sine ansatte.
vocabulary.note: In Danish business contexts 'direktør' usually means the top executive, not just any board-level director as in English.

vocabulary.example_from_article: én gang om året har han lønsamtaler med sine ansatte
vocabulary.note: Compound: 'løn' (wage/salary) + 'samtale' (conversation). A standard term in Danish workplace culture for the annual pay review meeting.

vocabulary.example_from_article: én gang om året har han lønsamtaler med sine ansatte
vocabulary.note: 'Ansat' functions as both adjective ('employed') and noun ('employee'). The plural definite 'de ansatte' is very common in formal and workplace writing.

vocabulary.example_from_article: Nu fortæller han om vejen til lønforhøjelse
Nu har han indvilliget i at fortælle om, hvad han mener
vocabulary.note: Formal/written register. Pattern: 'indvillige i at + infinitive'. More elevated than everyday 'sige ja til' or 'acceptere'.

vocabulary.example_from_article: Nu fortæller han om vejen til lønforhøjelse
vocabulary.note: 'Fortælle om' = to tell about something. Distinct from 'tale' (to speak in general); 'fortælle' implies conveying a story or information. Irregular past: fortalte.

vocabulary.example_from_article: hvad han mener, der virker for ansatte generelt
vocabulary.note: Dual meaning: 'det virker godt' = it works well; 'han virker træt' = he seems tired. Context determines which sense is intended.

vocabulary.english: thinks, believes, is of the opinion
vocabulary.example_from_article: hvad han mener, der virker for ansatte generelt
vocabulary.note: Expresses personal opinion. Do not confuse with 'betyde' (to mean/signify). 'Hvad mener du?' = 'What do you think?'

vocabulary.english: to refrain from, to stay away from, to avoid
vocabulary.example_from_article: hvad man skal holde sig fra at nævne
vocabulary.note: A fixed phrasal verb. 'Holde sig fra at + infinitive' = to refrain from doing something. The reflexive 'sig' is obligatory here.

vocabulary.example_from_article: hvad man skal holde sig fra at nævne
vocabulary.note: Regular verb. Slightly formal; common in both speech and writing. 'Det er værd at nævne' = 'It is worth mentioning'.

vocabulary.example_from_article: hvad han mener, der virker for ansatte generelt
vocabulary.note: Placed after the noun phrase it modifies here, but can also open a sentence: 'Generelt set er det svært.' Transparent cognate with English 'generally'.

grammar_notes.explanation: In 'hvad man skal holde sig fra at nævne', 'skal' expresses advice or obligation — what one should avoid mentioning. Danish modal verbs ('skal', 'må', 'kan', 'vil', 'bør') are always followed by a bare infinitive: 'man skal holde'. Note that the dependent phrase after the phrasal verb does take 'at': 'holde sig fra at nævne'.

grammar_notes.topic: V2 word order after a fronted adverb
grammar_notes.explanation: Danish main clauses follow the V2 rule: the finite verb must be in second position. When an adverb opens the clause, the subject and verb invert. Both sentences illustrate this: 'Nu har han indvilliget…' (adv → verb → subject) and 'Nu fortæller han…' (adv → verb → subject). Compare the neutral order: 'Han har indvilliget nu'.

'hvad han mener, der virker' is an indirect embedded clause meaning 'what he thinks works'. The interrogative 'hvad' introduces the indirect question, and the relative pronoun 'der' introduces the inner subject clause ('der virker' = 'that works'). This layered structure is common in written Danish and worth recognising at B1 level.

Danish forms the present perfect exactly as English does: auxiliary 'have' + past participle. 'Han har indvilliget' = 'He has agreed'. The participle 'indvilliget' ends in '-et', the regular weak form. This tense signals a past event with present relevance — here, that he has now agreed and is ready to share advice.

discussion_questions: Taler du eller din chef nogensinde om løn? Hvordan foregår det?
Hvad tror du, man IKKE skal sige i en lønsamtale? Giv et eksempel.
Synes du, det er vigtigt at have lønsamtaler én gang om året? Hvorfor eller hvorfor ikke?
title: Én gang om året taler han løn med sine ansatte. Nu fortæller han om vejen til lønforhøjelse
